method3.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island is an uninhabited coral island in the central equatorial Pacific Ocean, about 40 km (25 mi) south of the equator at 0°22′S 160°01′W, and is part of the central Line Islands, also known as the Teraina Islands.[2] The island is the exposed crest of an ancient coral reef cap over a massive extinct volcano, with a dried former lagoon at its center where deep guano deposits accumulated.[2] The emergent land area of the Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge is 1,273 acres, while submerged lands and waters within the three-mile limit add 36,214 acres.[3]

Jarvis has a tropical desert climate characterized by high daytime temperatures, steady easterly trade winds, intense equatorial sunlight, scant rainfall of less than 1 m (40 in) per year, and no known natural freshwater lens.[2] The terrain is mostly sandy and low-lying, reaching only about 7 m (23 ft) at its highest point.[2] Vegetation is sparse, consisting mainly of bunch grasses, vines, and low-growing shrubs, which provide nesting, roosting, and sheltering habitat for seabirds, shorebirds, and land hermit crabs.[2]

The waters around Jarvis are unusually productive because wind-driven equatorial upwelling combines with topographic upwelling of the eastward-flowing Equatorial Undercurrent.[2] Shallow marine habitats around the island include a fringing reef, shallow back reefs, a steep forereef, and a moderate-sized reef terrace on the eastern windward side.[2] The refuge has been described as a relatively intact tropical island ecosystem, with terrestrial, coral reef, and deep marine habitats that remain comparatively free of direct human impact.[3]

Jarvis supports more nesting seabirds than any other island in the U.S. tropical Pacific Islands.[2] The island has been identified as having 14 species of breeding seabirds, and its management has emphasized preservation of the complete terrestrial and marine ecosystem, especially the large seabird nesting colonies.[3] Jarvis alone has been estimated to support about one million sooty terns, making it one of the most important seabird nesting areas in the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[1] Past ecological disturbance included guano removal and invasive mammals such as cats and rats, but after eradication efforts seabird populations increased and shearwaters returned to Jarvis.[1]

==History==
The first known European sighting of Jarvis Island occurred on August 21, 1821, by the British ship *Eliza Francis*, which was owned by Edward, Thomas, and William Jarvis, after whom the island was named.[2] American whalers and other seafarers later noted guano on the island, and the United States Exploring Expedition surveyed Jarvis in 1840.[2]

In 1857, Arthur Benson and Charles Judd, aboard the Hawaiian schooner *Liholiho*, took possession of Jarvis and claimed it for the American Guano Company under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2] Commander Charles Henry Davis of the USS *St. Mary* subsequently surveyed the island and formally claimed it for the United States.[4] Guano deposits were worked from 1857 until 1879, when the island was abandoned after large quantities had been removed.[5] By the time of abandonment, about 8,000 tonnes of mined guano had reportedly been collected from the island.[1]

The United Kingdom annexed Jarvis in 1889 but did not develop further mining operations there.[4][5] In 1906, the island was leased to the Pacific Phosphate Company of London, although mining again was not developed.[4] The United States reoccupied and reclaimed Jarvis in 1935, and President Franklin D. Roosevelt’s Executive Order 7368 of May 13, 1936, placed its control and jurisdiction under the Secretary of the Interior.[3][4] During World War II, Japanese forces attacked the island in 1942, leading to the evacuation of its colonists.[1]

Jarvis was initially administered by the Office of Territorial Affairs, and on June 27, 1974, the Secretary of the Interior designated the island and its territorial sea out to 3 nautical miles as a unit of the National Wildlife Refuge System.[3] In 2009, Jarvis Island was also designated part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[5]

==Transportation==
Marine vessels capable of extended open-ocean voyages are the only means of transportation to Jarvis Island, which lies about 1,263 nautical miles from U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service management staff in Honolulu and takes about eight days to reach by ship.[3] In recent years, transportation has been provided by NOAA, the U.S. Coast Guard, and private charter vessels, often as part of multi-island scientific or management expeditions in the central Pacific.[3] Jarvis is commonly visited during comprehensive biennial NOAA scientific cruises that also include other remote Pacific island refuges such as Johnston, Howland, and Baker.[3] Transportation and field operations are constrained by the island’s remoteness, lack of reliable and affordable transport, limited infrastructure, extreme environmental conditions, and safety concerns associated with landing and operating there.[3] Public access to the refuge remains closed except for authorized management, research, or other permitted activities.[3] Supplies brought ashore for permitted expeditions, including fuel for generators, must be transported and stored under strict containment and spill-prevention requirements.[1]

==Military==
In the mid-1930s, Jarvis was included in the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, which placed U.S. citizens on Jarvis, Howland, and Baker so that weather stations and landing fields could be developed for military and commercial air routes between Australia and California.[1] The project also had a strategic purpose: strengthening U.S. claims to remote central Pacific islands as a check on Japanese territorial expansion.[1] The colonists, later known as Hui Panalāʻau, were mostly young men from Hawaii, including Native Hawaiian students and alumni, who rotated through Jarvis and the other islands before the program ended in early 1942.[1][2] Physical traces of this military-era use include short channels blasted through the shallow fringing reefs to improve small-boat access, particularly in the West and East Terrace areas.[4] Refuge planning documents note that hazards associated with human habitation during the island's guano-mining and military periods may still wash ashore or remain on the island.[3]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge is administered by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as part of the National Wildlife Refuge System, whose mission is to conserve, manage and, where appropriate, restore fish, wildlife, plants and their habitats for present and future generations.[2] At the time of its transfer to the Service, the refuge purpose was described as “the preservation of the complete ecosystem, terrestrial as well as marine,” with special emphasis on large seabird nesting colonies.[5] The original refuge boundary included all emergent land and the surrounding territorial sea to the three-mile limit, totaling 37,487 acres, of which 1,273 acres were land and 36,214 acres were submerged lands and waters.[5] Current protected-area and Fish and Wildlife Service descriptions list the refuge at about 429,853 acres, or approximately 1,744.87 km², with nearly all of that area consisting of submerged lands.[1][2] Refuge planning has been guided by a comprehensive conservation plan completed by the Fish and Wildlife Service in 2008, along with National Wildlife Refuge System goals for conserving species, habitats and habitat networks.[4][5] Management is conducted through periodic agency visits and activity reviews; a Fish and Wildlife Service biology team visit in August 2024 was described as the agency’s first trip to the refuge in more than six years.[3] The Service has also evaluated proposed public access through compatibility determinations, including a draft determination for amateur-radio operations that found the activity would not detract from protection of the refuge’s natural character if conducted under specified guidelines.[2]
